questions.question: What is a perfectionistic rule?

options.text: A helpful guideline for maintaining quality work
A rigid conditional belief that feels like an absolute truth but is actually a learned pattern
A standard set by your employer or organization
A moral principle about right and wrong

questions.explanation: Perfectionistic rules are rigid conditional beliefs — often in 'if... then' or 'I must...' format — that feel like absolute facts but are actually learned patterns. Examples include 'If I make a mistake, people will lose respect for me' or 'I must always produce outstanding work.' They can be questioned and changed.

questions.question: Which cognitive distortion involves focusing exclusively on the one imperfect element while ignoring everything that went well?

options.text: All-or-nothing thinking
Emotional reasoning
Mental filtering
Catastrophizing

questions.explanation: Mental filtering means selectively attending to the negative while filtering out the positive. For example, receiving 19 positive evaluations and one critical one, then ruminating exclusively about the criticism. This creates a distorted picture where imperfections dominate despite an overwhelmingly positive reality.

questions.question: What is the key difference between the inner critic and healthy self-evaluation?

options.text: The inner critic is always wrong while healthy self-evaluation is always right
Healthy self-evaluation focuses only on positives while the inner critic focuses only on negatives
The inner critic is global and punishing ('You are a failure') while healthy self-evaluation is specific and constructive ('That did not go as well as I hoped — I could try differently next time')
There is no real difference; they are the same process

questions.explanation: The inner critic makes global, permanent judgments about your worth ('You are a failure') and creates shame and withdrawal. Healthy self-evaluation is specific and temporary ('That did not go well'), focuses on behaviors rather than worth, can acknowledge what went well, and creates learning and motivation rather than punishment.

questions.question: The 'unrelenting standards' schema in perfectionism often developed from which type of early experience?

options.text: Being given too much freedom as a child
Growing up in families where love and acceptance were conditional on achievement
Having too many extracurricular activities
Being the youngest sibling

questions.explanation: The unrelenting standards schema — 'I must meet very high internalized standards of behavior and performance, usually to avoid criticism' — most commonly develops in families where love and acceptance felt conditional on achievement. When a child learns that approval depends on perfect performance, they internalize standards as a strategy for maintaining connection and safety.

questions.question: Why is mapping your personal perfectionism pattern useful?

options.text: It provides a diagnosis of your condition
It proves that your perfectionism is worse than other people's
It helps you identify your specific rules, distortions, critic patterns, and consequences so you know where intervention will be most effective
It is required before you can learn any coping techniques

questions.explanation: Mapping your personal pattern — your specific rules, cognitive distortions, inner critic tendencies, deeper beliefs, and consequences — helps you understand exactly how perfectionism operates in your life. This personalized understanding guides which strategies from later lessons will be most helpful and where to focus your efforts.

title: Perfectionistic Thinking: Rigid Rules and Self-Criticism - Quiz
